sections.text: Welcome back. Algebra One, Module Twelve — exponents and polynomials. Today we learn the five exponent rules that simplify everything, and the vocabulary for the expressions that drive most of higher algebra.

Take a single sheet of paper and fold it in half. Now imagine folding it in half fifty times. How thick is it? Try to guess. The answer is two to the fiftieth power. That's over a quadrillion sheets of paper, and the stack would be thicker than the distance from the Earth to the Sun. That's what exponents do. They don't grow. They explode.

Three sections today. One — five exponent rules: product, quotient, power of a power, zero, and negative. Two — polynomial vocabulary, the words you'll see on every test. Three — polynomial operations: adding, subtracting, and multiplying. Let's go.

First, the basics. X to the n means multiply x by itself n times. So x cubed equals x times x times x. The big number on the bottom is the base. The little number floating up top is the exponent. The exponent tells you how many copies to multiply together. Simple.

Product rule. X squared times x cubed. Write it out — that's x times x, times x times x times x. Five x's multiplied together, which is x to the fifth. The rule: same base, add the exponents. So two cubed times two to the fourth equals two to the seventh. You don't multiply the exponents — you add them.

Quotient rule. X to the fifth divided by x squared. Five x's on top, two x's on bottom. Two cancel and you're left with three x's, which is x cubed. Rule: same base, subtract the exponents. Seven to the eighth divided by seven squared equals seven to the sixth. Top minus bottom.

Power of a power. X squared, all raised to the third. That means take x squared and multiply it by itself three times. Each one contributes two x's, so you get six total. X to the sixth. Rule: when you raise a power to a power, multiply the exponents.

Two special cases. Anything nonzero raised to the zero is one. Yes, even five to the zero is one. And a negative exponent means take the reciprocal. Two to the negative three is one over two cubed, which is one eighth. Negative exponent just flips it to the denominator.

Your turn. Simplify x to the fifth, times x to the negative two, all divided by x cubed. Apply the product rule first, then the quotient rule. Combine the exponents carefully. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you're ready.

Time. On top: x to the fifth times x to the negative two — add the exponents, five plus negative two equals three, so x cubed. Now divide: x cubed over x cubed — subtract three minus three, that's x to the zero, which equals one. The final answer is one. Beautiful.

Polynomial vocabulary. A polynomial is a sum of terms, where each term is a coefficient times a variable raised to a whole-number exponent. The degree is the highest exponent. The leading coefficient is the number in front of that highest-degree term. One term is called a monomial, two terms a binomial, three terms a trinomial. Memorize these words — every textbook uses them.

Adding and subtracting polynomials means combining like terms. Like terms have the exact same variable and exponent. Three x squared plus two x minus one, plus x squared minus four x plus five. Group: three x squared plus x squared is four x squared. Two x minus four x is negative two x. Negative one plus five is four. Final answer: four x squared minus two x plus four. For subtraction, distribute the minus sign first.

Multiplying a monomial by a polynomial — use the distributive law. Three x times the quantity two x squared plus five x minus four. Hit every term inside with three x. Three x times two x squared is six x cubed. Three x times five x is fifteen x squared. Three x times negative four is negative twelve x. Done.

The single biggest trap students fall into. You can only add exponents when you multiply same-base terms. X squared plus x cubed is NOT x to the fifth. They're different exponents, so they're not like terms — you can't combine them at all. X squared plus x cubed just stays as x squared plus x cubed. Adding bases and adding exponents are different operations.

Recap. Five exponent rules — product adds, quotient subtracts, power of a power multiplies, zero gives one, and negative flips to the denominator. Polynomial vocab — degree, leading coefficient, monomial, binomial, trinomial. Add and subtract by combining like terms. Multiply a monomial across a polynomial using distribution. Your assignment is in the dashboard.

Reminder — this video is the overview. To actually master this, here's the rest. Read OpenStax Chapter six point two on properties of exponents, and section six point three on multiplying polynomials. Do the Khan Academy sets on exponent properties and polynomial operations. The dashboard assignment has ten exponent problems and four polynomial problems. Book an advisor check-in if negative exponents still feel weird. Next up — Module Thirteen — factoring polynomials.
